analytic.title: VASP2KP: k โข p models and Landรฉ g-factors from ab initio calculations

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: The k โข p method is significant in condensed matter physics for the compact and analytical Hamiltonian. In the presence of magnetic field, it is described by the effective Zeeman's coupling Hamiltonian with Landรฉ g-factors. Here, we develop an open-source package VASP2KP (including two parts: vasp2mat and mat2kp) to compute k โข p parameters and Landรฉ g-factors directly from the wavefunctions provided by the density functional theory (DFT) as implemented in Vienna ab initio Simulation Package (VASP). First, we develop a VASP patch vasp2mat to compute matrix representations of the generalized momentum operator ฯ = p + 1 2mc 2 (ล ร โV (r)), spin operator ล, time reversal operator T and crystalline symmetry operators R on the DFT wavefunctions. Second, we develop a python code mat2kp to obtain the unitary transformation U that rotates the degenerate DFT basis towards the standard basis, and then automatically compute the k โข p parameters and g-factors. The theory and the methodology behind VASP2KP are described in detail. The matrix elements of the operators are derived comprehensively and computed correctly within the projector augmented wave method. We apply this package to some materials, e.g. Bi2Se3, Na3Bi, Te, InAs and 1H-TMD monolayers. The obtained effective model's dispersions are in good agreement with the DFT data around the specific wave vector, and the g-factors are consistent with experimental data.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: Electronic band structures hold immense importance in the field of condensed matter physics and materials science, providing crucial insights into the behavior of electrons within crystalline materials. They reveals information about distributions of energy levels, energy gaps, and density of states, which are essential for determining material electrical conductivities, optical properties, thermal behavior, and so on. In this background, the density functional theory (DFT) [1,2] was developed, which gave birth to many first-principles calculation softwares or codes based on them, including VASP [3,4], Quantum Espresso [5,6], CASTEP [7], ABINIT [8,9], and so on [10][11][12][13].

However, DFT bands are quite complex, which contain quite a large number of energy bands that hardly affect the desired physical properties thus making the physical pictures difficult to understand. In order to construct a model that only involves a few bands with a greater impact on the physical properties of materials, the k โข p method is proposed, which is used to construct an effective model to describe the quasiparticles near the specific wave vector in the reciprocal space [14]. The k โข p models constructed through the theory of invariants are analytical and only contain a few important bands, thus making the physical pictures quite clear. There are some arbitrary parameters in k โข p models, which could be determined by fitting to the corresponding experimental data or the DFT calculation data such as band structures. The k โข p method has been successfully applied to many condensed-matter systems, including metals [15,16], semiconductors [17,18], topological insulators and superconductors [19][20][21][22], spin-lasers [23,24], nanostructured solids [25], two-dimensional van der Waals materials [26,27] and so on [28][29][30][31].

When a magnetic field is applied to a condensed matter system, we can use effective Zeeman's coupling Hamiltonian to describe the effects of the magnetic field. Effective Zeeman's coupling determines the split of Kramers states under magnetic field, leading to outcomes like the Pauli paramagnetism of the metals and the van Vleck paramagnetism of insulators. The important parameters in Zeeman's coupling are Landรฉ g-factors. Landรฉ g-factors of materials have been widely studied in quantum wires [32][33][34][35], quantum dots [36,37], semiconductors nanostructures [38][39][40][41][42], topological materials [43,44] and so on [45][46][47]. Moreover, the theory of invariants can also be used to construct Zeeman's coupling Hamiltonian conveniently. However, there is no available code to compute Landรฉ g-factors and k โข p parameters directly from DFT wavefunctions.

First of all, Gao et al. developed the program IRVSP to determine the irreducible representations (irreps) arXiv:2312.08729v1 [cond-mat.mtrl-sci] 14 Dec 2023 of bands at any k-point in VASP calculations [48]. Then, Jiang et al. developed the python package kdotp-generator to generate the k โข p effective Hamiltonian for the given irreps automatically [49]. Furthermore, Song et al. derived effective Zeeman's coupling and g-factors in DFT calculations [50]. Thus, it is straightforward for us to generate the VASP2KP code to construct the effective models and to compute the model parameters from VASP wavefunctions directly. Although many similar codes are developed subsequently for Quantum Espresso, such as IR2PW [51], IrRep [52], and DFT2kp [53], their functions do not go beyond the above mentioned codes. The DFT2kp can not generate the Zeeman's coupling Hamiltonians. Additionally, the matrix elements of symmetry operators are not obtained properly, because the projector augmented wave (PAW) corrections are neglected in DFT2kp.

In this work, we have developed an open-source package VASP2KP, which can generate the effective k โข p model and Zeeman's coupling, and obtain the values of the k โข p parameters and g-factors. This package contains two parts: a VASP patch vasp2mat and a post-processing python code mat2kp. First, we use vasp2mat to generate matrix representations for generalized momentum ฯ = p + 1 2mc 2 (ล ร โV (r)), spin ล, time reversal T , and crystalline symmetry operators R in DFT calculations. The matrix elements of the space group operators are derived in detail and computed correctly in the PAW wavefunctions. Second, mat2kp can obtain the unitary transformation U from the degenerate wavefunctions to the k โข p standard basis, and then compute these parameters automatically. The obtained effective masses and g-factors are important and comparable with experimental observations. The paper is organized as the following. In Sec. II, the theoretical foundations behind VASP2KP are introduced. In Sec. III the main algorithm steps are described in detail. The results obtained by VASP2KP are shown and analysed for some typical materials in Sec. IV, and we have some discussions in Sec. V.

div.head: II. THEORY AND METHODOLOGY
div.p: In this section, we first introduce the k โข p method in Sec. II A. The method to obtain Zeeman's coupling is presented in Sec. II B. Then the theory of invariants is reviewed and the invariant k โข p Hamiltonian as well as Zeeman's coupling are obtained in Sec. II C. In Sec. II D, we propose a general routine to get the unitary transformation that change degenerate DFT basis to the k โข p standard basis. Last, the method to calculate k โข p parameters and g-factors is introduced in Sec. II E.

div.head: A. k โข p effective Hamiltonian
div.p: When we only care about wave vector K around a specific wave vector k 0 in the Brillouin zone, it can be well described by using the k โข p effective model. Suppose that ฯ nK (r) is the wavefunction of n-th band which satisfies the Schrรถdinger equation with spin-orbit coupling (SOC), i.e., ฤคB ฯ nK (r) = ฯต n (K)ฯ nK (r), where
div.formula: ฤคB = p2 2m + V (r) + 1 2m 2 c 2 (ล ร โV (r)) โข p (1)
div.p: is the Bloch Hamiltonian operator with SOC, and p is the momentum operator, V (r) is the potential in crystal, ล is the spin momentum operator, m is the electron mass, and c is the light speed in vacuum. To expand the Hamiltonian at k 0 , introducing a transformation ฯ nk (r) = ฯ nK (r)e -ikโขr , where k = Kk 0 is the deviation from k 0 , the Schrรถdinger equation which
div.formula: ฯ k (r) obeys is ฤคB + โ 2 k 2 2m + ฤคโฒ (k) ฯ nk (r) = ฯต n (K)ฯ nk (r) (2)
div.p: where ฤคโฒ (k) = โ m kโขฯ is the first-order term, and ฯ = p+
is the generalized momentum operator with SOC. We can take ฤคkp = ฤคB + โ 2 k 2 2m + ฤคโฒ (k) as the equivalent Hamiltonian of ฤคB since the eigenvalues of them are all equal.

Suppose that we have got the eigenenergies {ฯต n (k 0 )} and the eigenstates {|n(k 0 )โฉ} of ฤคB . Then we can obtain the generalized momentum elements by ฯ mn = โจm(k 0 )| ฯ|n(k 0 )โฉ. Thus the matrix elements of ฤคkp can be obtained by
div.formula: H kp-all mn (k) = ฯต n (k 0 ) + โ 2 k 2 2m ฮด mn + โ m ฯ mn โข k (3)
div.p: Usually, we aimed at several low-energy bands (the set of which is denoted as A). The set of other bands is denoted as B. Then we can fold down the H kp-all mn (k) Hamiltonian into a subspace A via Lรถwdin partitioning. After twoorder Lรถwdin partitioning, the effective k โข p Hamiltonian is transformed as (see Appendix A for details)
div.formula: H kp ฮฑฮฒ (k) = ฯต ฮฑ (k 0 ) + โ 2 k 2 2m ฮด ฮฑฮฒ + โ m ฯ ฮฑฮฒ โข k + โ 2 2m 2 lโB ij ฯ i ฮฑl ฯ j lฮฒ k i k j ร 1 ฯต ฮฑ (k 0 ) -ฯต l (k 0 ) + 1 ฯต ฮฒ (k 0 ) -ฯต l (k 0 ) (4)
div.p: with i, j โ {x, y, z}. Hereafter we use ฮฑ, ฮฒ โ A, l โ B and m, n โ A โช B. Moreover, the k โข p Hamiltonian of the third order can be constructed similarly in Appendix A.

When a magnetic field is applied to the system, the momenta โk i should be replaced by (-iโโ i + eA i ) according to Peierls substitution, where A is the vector potential and e (positively valued) is the elementary charge. Therefore, โ 2 k i k j in the summation will be replaced by the sum of the gauge dependent term 1 2 -iโโ i + eA i , -iโโ j + eA j and gauge independent term 1 2 -iโโ i + eA i , -iโโ j + eA j = -iโe 2 k ฯต ijk B k , where B is the magnetic induction intensity. In this case, the total Hamiltonian can be written by the summation of the
div.formula: H kp ฮฑฮฒ =ฯต ฮฑ (k 0 )ฮด ฮฑฮฒ + โ m ฯ ฮฑฮฒ โข -iโ + e โ A + ij M ij ฮฑฮฒ -iโ i + e โ A i -iโ j + e โ A j ,(5)
div.p: where
div.formula: M ij ฮฑฮฒ = โ 2 2m ฮด ฮฑฮฒ ฮด ij + โ 2 4m 2 lโB ฯ i ฮฑl ฯ j lฮฒ + ฯ j ฮฑl ฯ i lฮฒ ร 1 ฯต ฮฑ (k 0 ) -ฯต l (k 0 ) + 1 ฯต ฮฒ (k 0 ) -ฯต l (k 0 ) .(6)
div.p: Zeeman's coupling term can be expressed by
div.formula: H Z ฮฑฮฒ = ยต B โ (L ฮฑฮฒ + 2s ฮฑฮฒ ) โข B,(7)
div.p: where
div.formula: L k ฮฑฮฒ = - iโ 2m lโB ij ฯต ijk ฯ i ฮฑl ฯ j lฮฒ ร 1 ฯต ฮฑ (k 0 ) -ฯต l (k 0 ) + 1 ฯต ฮฒ (k 0 ) -ฯต l (k 0 ) (8)
div.p: can be considered as the orbital contribution, s ฮฑฮฒ = โจฮฑ(k 0 )|ล|ฮฒ(k 0 )โฉ are the spin elements, and ยต B is the Bohr magneton. The detailed derivation is shown in Appendix B.

div.head: C. Theory of invariants
div.p: Suppose that the little group at k 0 is L, and D(R) is a representation (rep) for โR โ L, whose dimension is N . There are a total of N 2 independent Hermitian matrices
div.formula: X = X i (i = 1, 2, โข โข โข , N 2 ) in N dimensions that consti- tute an N -dimensional matrix space. It is simple to get a new rep D (X) by D(R)X n D(R) -1 = m X m D (X) mn (R)(9)
div.p: for โR โ L. Also, we can construct polynomial space of order p โ N, whose basis can be chosen as g
div.formula: (k) = {k i x k j y k l z |i + j + l โค p, i, j, l โ N}. It is simple to get a new rep D (g) by Rg n (k) = g n (R -1 k) = m g m (k)D (g) mn (R). (10
div.p: Spatial inversion P and time reversal T may also be the elements of the little group L, which transform the wave vector as k P -โ -k and k T -โ -k, respectively. Decomposing D (X) and D (g) into irreps, we have
div.formula: D (X) (R) โ ฮป a ฮป D ฮป (R)(11)
D (g) (R) โ ฮป b ฮป D ฮป (R)(12)
div.p: where D ฮป represents irreps and a ฮป or b ฮป denotes the multiplicities of D ฮป . Note that the irreps decomposed from the D (X) and D (g) are not exactly the same. It is simple to get the matrix basis X ฮป,ยต and polynomial basis g ฮป,ฮฝ (k) of D ฮป -irrep. An irrep may correspond to more than one sets of matrix basis or polynomial basis, thus enabling us to add an indicator ยต/ฮฝ to distinguish them. After obtaining matrix basis and polynomial basis, the k โข p Hamiltonian can be written as
div.formula: H kp (k) = ฮป a ฮป ยต=1 b ฮป ฮฝ=1 c ฮปยตฮฝ X ฮป,ยต โข g * ฮป,ฮฝ (k),(13)
div.p: where c ฮปยตฮฝ are undetermined kโขp parameters, which must be real [49]. It can be proved that the p-th order k โข p Hamiltonian expressed by Eq. ( 13) satisfies the relation
div.formula: H kp ( Rk) = D(R)H kp (k)D(R) -1(14)
div.p: for โR โ L [49]. Moreover, in the presence of magnetic field, Zeeman's coupling can also be constructed in the same way. Unlike the wave vector k, the magnetic field B is a pseudovector, thus making it satisfy the relation B P -โ B and B T -โ -B under spatial inversion and time reversal, respectively. Effective Zeeman's coupling satisfies the relation
div.formula: H Z ( RB) = D(R)H Z (B)D(R) -1(15)
div.p: By the way, given the matrix representations of the generators of the little group L, the python package kdotp-generator constructs the standard k โข p Hamiltonian or Zeeman's coupling with undetermined k โข p parameters as Eq. ( 13). The standard matrices D std (R) are given on the Bilbao Crystalline Server (BCS) for the assigned irreps by IRVSP .

div.head: D. Unitary transformation
div.p: In DFT calculations, when the irrep is n-fold (n โฅ 2) and the eigenstates are degenerate, there is U (n) ambiguity in the DFT eigenstates. In this section, we propose a general routine to get the unitary transformation that changes degenerate DFT wavefunctions to the k โข p standard basis. We can obtain the eigenenergies {ฯต n (k 0 )} and eigenstates {|n(k 0 )โฉ} in VASP. The matrix representation of R under the VASP basis set would be calculated by mat2kp, which is denoted as D num (R).
However, D num (R) is usually not in the standard form; they are related by a unitary transformation, i.e.,
div.formula: D std (R) = U โ D num (R)U (16
div.p: for โR โ L, where U is a unitary matrix. To find U , it is obvious that only the generators of the k 0 -little group L should be taken into account. They are space group operators {R t |v t }, consisting of rotational part and translational part. The translational part is usually expressed by a phase factor. The Eq. ( 16) can be rewritten as
div.formula: D num (R)U -U D std (R) = 0 (17
div.p: where 0 is a zero matrix. The real part and the imaginary part of each elements of U are independent variables, which are denoted as 17), it is clear to find that all these variables satisfy a linear equation set. Using a column vector
div.formula: U r11 , U r12 , โข โข โข U rnn and U i11 , U i12 , โข โข โข U inn , respectively. From Eq. (
u = (U r11 , U r12 , โข โข โข , U rnn , U i11 , U i12 , โข โข โข , U inn ) T , the
div.p: Eq. ( 17) can be rewritten as
where Q is a real coefficient matrix defined by Eq. ( 17).
The details are shown in Appendix C. Therefore, all vectors in the null space of the matrix Q is the solution to Eq. ( 16). Perform singular value decomposition on matrix Q, we can obtain
div.formula: Q = V 1 ฮฃV T 2 thus QV 2 = V 1 ฮฃ
div.p: , where V 1 and V 2 are orthogonal matrices and ฮฃ is a diagonal matrix. It can be proved that the column vectors in V 1 correspond to the singular value 0 form the basis of the null space of the matrix Q, which are denoted as {u 1 , u 2 , โข โข โข , u m }. After reshaping these vectors, the basis of the solution space of U of Eq. ( 16) are denoted as
div.formula: {U 1 , U 2 , โข โข โข , U n }. Any solution can be written as U = m ฮฑ=1 ฮป ฮฑ U ฮฑ (19
div.p: where ฮป ฮฑ must be real. Thus, one has to get one set of the parameters {ฮป ฮฑ } to satisfy U โ U = I, where I is an identity matrix. However, this relation is nonlinear, thus making it hard to solve by treating it as an equation directly. In mat2kp, the unitary U is obtained via the sequential least squares programming to find optimal parameter set {ฮป ฮฑ }, which can minimize the error ฮต = ij (U โ U -I) ij .

div.head: E. Calculations of parameters
div.p: Applying Eqs. (4)(5)(6)(7)(8) in DFT calculations, one can get the numerical Hamiltonian H num , one have to solve the equation H std = U -1 H num U to get all the parameters in H std . By the way, from Eq. ( 13), it is easy to find that the equations are all linear equations. However, there are more equations than parameters. The coefficients of each linear equation are not accurate because of numerical errors generated from VASP calculations, making it impossible to solve by the traditional Gaussian elimination method. We can write all equations in a matrix form
where A is a constant matrix, and x is the column vector comprised of all undetermined real parameters.

To eliminate the constraint of the real x, suppose that A = A r + iA i and b = b r + ib i , where the subscript r represents the real part and the subscript i represents the imaginary part. Then Eq. ( 20) can be transformed as
div.formula: A r A i x = b r b i .(21)
div.p: In this case, all the matrices become real (the real constraint is automatically satisfied). Therefore, the linear least square method can be used to calculate the parameters and to give the error as well.

div.head: III. CAPABILITY OF VASP2KP
div.p: The VASP2KP package contains two parts: the VASP patch vasp2mat and the post-processing python code mat2kp. The workflow is presented in Fig. 1. The methodology of obtaining the matrix elements of the generalized momentum, spin, time reversal and crystalline rotational operators in VASP is introduced in Sec. III A. The main algorithm steps of VASP2KP are described in Sec. III B. Lastly, main steps to construct k โข p models and Zeeman's coupling via VASP2KP are shown in Sec. III C.

div.head: A. vasp2mat: to compute matrix elements from DFT wavefunctions
div.p: The matrix elements of ฯ, ล, T and R are required in constructing the k โขp Hamiltonian and Zeeman's coupling under the DFT wavefunctions as shown in Sec. II A. The computation method of ฯ matrix elements was first introduced in Ref. [50]. Here we generalize it to nonlocal operators. In VASP, the PAW potential is used, which is a combination and generalization of the linear augmented plane wave method. Therefore, we derive these matrix elements under the PAW wavefunctions, which are implemented in vasp2mat to compute the matrix elements.
The relation of all electron wavefunction (|n(k 0 )โฉ) and pseudo wavefunction
div.formula: (| n(k 0 )โฉ) in PAW is |n(k 0 )โฉ = T | n(k 0 )โฉ ,(22)
div.p: where the linear transformation T can be expressed as [54,55]
div.formula: T = 1 + aยตฮถ ฯ a ยต ฮถ -ฯ a ยต ฮถ p a ยต ฮถ .(23)
div.p: The ฯ a ยต ฮถ is the direct product of the real space all electron partial wavefunction ฯ a ยต (r) at the a-th atom with a spinor wavefunction |ฮถโฉ (ฮถ =โ or โ), ฯ a ยต ฮถ is the direct product of the real space pseudo partial wavefunction ฯ a ยต (r) with a spinor wavefunction |ฮถโฉ, and p a ยต ฮถ is a projector wavefunction comprised of the direct product of a real space projector wavefunction p a ยต (r) and a spinor wavefunction |ฮถโฉ. Here, ฯ a ยต (r)'s are obtained by all-electron calculation for the reference atom. The pseudo partial wavefunctions ฯ a ยต (r) are identical to ฯ a ยต (r) outside the augmentation sphere of the corresponding atom and are much softer than ฯ a ยต (r) inside the augmentation sphere; ฯ a ยต (r)'s provide a complete basis for the pseudo wavefunction | n(k 0 )โฉ inside the augmentation sphere. The projector wavefunctions are defined in such a way, i.e., โจ
div.formula: p a ยต | ฯ a ยต โฒ โฉ = ฮด ยตยต โฒ , that โจ p a ยต ฮถ| n(k 0 )โฉ gives the expanding coefficients of | n(k 0 )โฉ on ฯ a ยต (r).
div.p: Usually the projector wavefunctions are chosen to be zero outside the core radius. Therefore, outside the augmentation sphere the third and second terms in T cancel each other exactly, and inside the augmentation sphere the first and third terms cancel each other exactly. T leaves | n(k 0 )โฉ unchanged outside the augmentation sphere and maps it to the all-electron wavefunction inside the augmentation sphere. Both ฯ a ยต (r) and ฯ a ยต (r) are stored as a radial part times an angular part, which can be expressed as
div.formula: ฯ a ยต (r) = Y mยต lยต ( r -ฯ a )R a ยต (|r -ฯ a |) ฯ a ยต (r) = Y mยต lยต ( r -ฯ a ) R a ยต (|r -ฯ a |)(24)
div.p: where ฯ a is the site of the a-th atom, Y m l are sphere harmonics, and R a ยต are real functions. The hatted vectors represent the unit vector along the corresponding directions.
According to Eq. ( 23), the PAW matrix form of a local operator F can be expressed by
div.formula: F mn = โจm(k 0 )| F |n(k 0 )โฉ = โจ m(k 0 )|T โ F T | n(k 0 )โฉ = โจ m(k 0 )| F | n(k 0 )โฉ + aยตฮฝ ฮถฮถ โฒ m(k 0 ) p a ยต ฮถ F a ยตฮถ,ฮฝฮถ โฒ โจ p a ฮฝ ฮถ โฒ | n(k 0 )โฉ ,(25)
div.p: where F a ยตฮถ,ฮฝฮถ โฒ is the projection matrix of the operator F in the a-th atom's augmentation sphere, which is defined as
div.formula: F a ยตฮถ,ฮฝฮถ โฒ = ฯ a ยต ฮถ F ฯ a ฮฝ ฮถ โฒ -ฯ a ยต ฮถ F ฯ a ฮฝ ฮถ โฒ . (26
div.p: The first term in F a ยตฮถ,ยต โฒ ฮถ โฒ gives the contribution from the all-electron wavefunction in the augmentation sphere, and the second term cancels the contribution from the pseudo wavefunction in the augmentation sphere that is counted in the first term of Eq. (25).
In PAW, the plane wave is used to span the pseudo wavefunction ฯ nk0 (r, ฮถ) = โจr, ฮถ| n(k 0 )โฉ, which is expressed by
div.formula: ฯ nk0 (r, ฮถ) = G c nk0 ฮถG e i(k0+G)โขr(27)
div.p: where c nk0 ฮถG are the plane wave coefficients. The projection coefficients p a ยต ฮถ n(k 0 ) in the second term of Eq. ( 25) have already been calculated in VASP. Therefore, to calculate the matrix of the local operator F , we need to calculate โจ m(k 0 )| F | n(k 0 )โฉ and F a ยตฮถ,ฮฝฮถ โฒ in Eq. ( 25).

div.head: Generalized momentum matrix
div.p: The matrix of the generalized momentum ฯ can be expressed by substituting ฯ into F in Eq. ( 25) as follows:
div.formula: ฯ mn = โจ m(k 0 )| p| n(k 0 )โฉ + 1 2mc 2 โจ m(k 0 )|ล ร โV | n(k 0 )โฉ + aยตฮฝ ฮถฮถ โฒ m(k 0 ) p a ยต ฮถ p a ยตฮถ,ฮฝฮถ โฒ โจ p a ฮฝ ฮถ โฒ | n(k 0 )โฉ + 1 2mc 2 aยตฮฝฮถฮถ โฒ m(k 0 ) p a ยต ฮถ ฯ a ยต ฮถ ล ร โV ฯ a ฮฝ ฮถ โฒ โจ p a ฮฝ ฮถ โฒ | n(k 0 )โฉ - 1 2mc 2 aยตฮฝฮถฮถ โฒ m(k 0 ) p a ยต ฮถ ฯ a ยต ฮถ ล ร โV ฯ a ฮฝ ฮถ โฒ โจ p a ฮฝ ฮถ โฒ | n(k 0 )โฉ (28)
div.p: Since the SOC effect is considered only within the augmentation spheres in VASP, where | n(k 0 )โฉ = aยตฮถ ฯ a ยต ฮถ p a ยต ฮถ n(k 0 ) , we can make the second term and the fifth term in Eq. ( 28) cancel out. Therefore, the generalized momentum matrix can be simplified to

div.head: Space group operator matrices
div.p: The general (magnetic) space group operator (SGO) is expressed by F = {R|v} or F = {T R|v}, which is usually a nonlocal (NL) operator. One should notice that the SGO commutes with T in Eq. (23). Thus the matrix element can be written as
div.formula: F N L mn = โจm(k 0 )| F |n(k 0 )โฉ = m(k 0 ) T โ T F โข n(k 0 ) =โจ m(k 0 )| F โข n(k 0 )โฉ + aยตฮฝฮถ m(k 0 ) p a ยต ฮถ C a ยต,ฮฝ p a ฮฝ ฮถ F โข n(k 0 ) ,(35)

div.head: B. The python code mat2kp
div.p: In this subsection, we give a brief introduction of the main algorithm steps of mat2kp. This code needs the inputs of ฮต n (k 0 ), ฯ mn , s ฮฑฮฒ , D num ฮฑฮฒ (R) and D std ฮฑฮฒ (R), which correspond to the EIGENVAL, MAT Pi.m, MAT sig.m, MAT R.m and mat2kp.in files, respectively. The main steps of mat2kp are as follows:
1. Following Sec.

II D, calculate the unitary transformation matrix The general workflow is given in Fig. 1. We will take Bi 2 Se 3 as an example for illustration. 3. Run vasp2mat to generate ฯ mn (vmat=11; vmat name='Pi'), s ฮฑฮฒ (vmat=10; vmat name='sig'), and generators' [D(R) num ฮฑฮฒ ; vmat=12] matrices directly from the VASP wavefunctions (WAVECAR) by the following settings in "INCAR.mat" files, respectively. These numerical matrix representations are output in MAT Pi.m, MAT sig.m, MAT R.m files. They are in the 'vasp-MAT' folder (given in mat2kp.in). div.head: IV. APPLICATIONS IN MATERIALS
div.p: In this section, we apply this package to some typical materials, i.e., Bi 2 Se 3 , Na 3 Bi, Te, InAs, and 1H-TMD, to construct the effective models and to compute all the parameters.

div.head: A. Four-band model at ฮ in Bi2Se3
div.p: As we know, the topological property of Bi 2 Se 3 is due to the band inversion at ฮ. By using IRVSP, the lowest conduction band belongs to GM9 (twofold degenerate), while the highest valence band belongs to GM8 (twofold degenerate), as depicted in Fig. 2(a). Based on these states in the ascending order, the low-energy effective Hamiltonian at ฮ is constructed automatically. The generators of the ฮ-little group are {C 3z |0, 0, 0}, {C 2x |0, 0, 0}, {P |0, 0, 0}, and {T |0, 0, 0}. Their standard matrix representations are given in TABLE I, which are needed by the code mat2kp to construct effective models.

To the second order, the kโขp Hamiltonian and Zeeman's coupling are given in Eq. (37), where ยต B is the Bohr magneton (โผ 0.05788 meV/Tesla). After their numerical matrix representations are computed by vasp2mat directly from the VASP wavefunctions, the parameters of k โข p Hamiltonian and g-factors of Zeeman's coupling are computed, as shown in model's dispersions are plotted in Fig. 2(b). They fit well with the VASP bands in the vicinity of ฮ. Moreover, the dispersions of the all-band k โข p model without downfolding in Eq. ( 3) are also plotted for comparison (labeled by 'kp-all').

div.p: C. Four-band model at H in Te

Element tellurium is a narrow-gap semiconductor. The direct gap is at H. The low-energy bands at H are the H4irrep and H5-irrep valence bands and H6-irrep (doubly degenerate) conduction bands. The four-band effective model is constructed accordingly. The standard matrix representations of the generators of the H-little group are given in TABLE E2. The k โข p model at H is expressed as
Zeeman's coupling is expressed by

In 1H-phase transition metal chalcogenide (TMD) monolayers, their direct gaps are at K. The two valence bands at K belong to the K8-irrep and K11-irrep, respectively. The standard matrix representations of the generators of the K-little group are given in TABLE E4. The two-band effective models are constructed in Eq. ( 42) (to the second order), which are plotted in Fig. 4. The computed k โข p parameters and g-factors are listed in TA-BLE VI.
V. DISCUSSION

In this work, we develop an open-source package VASP2KP to construct the k โข p Hamiltonian and Zeeman's coupling, and to compute the k โข p parameters and Landรฉ g-factors from the VASP calculations directly. By applying this package in many typical materials, we get k โข p effective models, whose band dispersions are in good agreement with the VASP data around the specific wave vector in the Brillouin zone. As the orbital contribution between the low-energy bands is usually remarkable (due to the small energy difference), the computed gfactor is not accurate in the multiple-band model. Thus, we recompute the effective g-factor for the two conduction bands only in Bi 2 Se 3 and Te. The recomputed values are mainly consistent with the previous experimental data listed in TABLE VII. We reveal that the conduction bands of Te have the larger g-factor than the valence bands, and the 1st valence band has the larger g-factor than the 2nd valence band in 1H-TMD monolayers. Our results indicate that VASP2KP has the capabilities to handle more materials.
div.formula: ฮ M K ฮ E-E (eV) ฮ M K ฮ E-E (eV) ฮ M K ฮ E-E (eV) (a) (b) (c)(d)
div.p: The obtained models provide the effective masses and g-factors, which are important physical quantities of the materials. The minor discrepancy from the experimental data is due to the non-precise band gap in the DFT calculations. To improve these parameters, one can use our code in the hybrid functionals or GW calculations. For the new synthesized or predicted materials, for which there is no experimental data available, our code can be used to predict reliable parameters. In conclusion, VASP2KP [60] would be widely used in the Materials Science. and the perturbation terms

By downfolding processing and using Eqs.(5)(6)(7)(8), get the numerical k โข p Hamiltonian and Zeeman's coupling and compute the coefficients numerically under DFT basis, i.e., H kp-num and H Z-num . Through theory of invariants in Sec. II C, import the package kdotp-generator and generate the standard k โข p Hamiltonian and Zeeman's coupling with a set of undetermined parameters, H kp-std and H Z-std , respectively.4. Obtain the values of k โข p parameters and Landรฉg-factors by solving H kp-std = U -1 H kp-num U andH Z-std = U -1 H Z-num U in Sec. II E.C. Run the post-processing python code mat2kp with the input files, to construct the standard kโขp Hamiltonian and Zeeman's coupling, and compute the values of kโขp parameters and g-factors. The outputs are "kp-parameters.out" and "g-factors.out" files, as pasted below. ######## kp-parameters.out ######## kp Hamiltonian ========== Result of kp Hamiltonian ========== Matrix([[a1 + a2 + c1*(kx**2 + ky**2) + c2*(kx**2 + ky**2) + c3*kz**2 + c4*kz**2, 0, -I*b2*kz, -b1*(kx*(sqrt(3) + 3*I) + ky*(3 -sqrt(3)*I))/3], [0, a1 + a2 + c1*(kx**2 + ky**2) + c2*(kx**2 + ky**2) + c3*kz**2 + c4*kz**2, b1*(kx*(sqrt(3) -3*I) + ky*(3 + sqrt(3)*I))/3, I*b2*kz], [I*b2*kz, b1*(kx*(sqrt(3) + 3*I) + ky*(3 -sqrt(3)*I))/3, a1 -a2 + c1*(kx**2 + ky**2) -c2*(kx**2 + ky**2) + c3*kz**2 -c4*kz**2, 0], [-b1*(kx*(sqrt(3) -3*I) + ky*(3 + sqrt(3)*I))/3, -I*b2*kz, 0, a1 -a2 + c1*(kx**2 + ky**2) -c2*(kx**2 + ky**2) + c3*kz**2 -c4*kz**2Error of the linear least square method: 3.93e-06 Sum of absolute values of numerical zero elements: 6.47e-02 ######## g-factors.out ######## Zeeman's coupling ========== Result of Zeeman's coupling ========== mu B/2*Matrix([[Bz*g3 + Bz*g4, g1*(Bx*(1 -sqrt(3)*I/3) + By*(-sqrt(3)/3 -I)) + g2*(Bx*(1 -sqrt(3)*I/3) + By*(-sqrt(3)/3 -I)), 0, 0], [g1*(Bx*(1 + sqrt(3)*I/3) + By*(-sqrt(3)/3 + I)) + g2*(Bx*(1 + sqrt(3)*I/3) + By*(-sqrt(3)/3 + I)), -Bz*g3 -Bz*g4, 0, 0], [0, 0, Bz*g3 -Bz*g4, g1*(Bx*(1 -sqrt(3)*I/3) + By*(-sqrt(3)/3 -I)) + g2*(Bx*(-1 + sqrt(3)*I/3) + By*(sqrt(3)/3 + I))], [0, 0, g1*(Bx*(1 + sqrt(3)*I/3) + By*(-sqrt(3)/3 + I)) + g2*(Bx*(-1 -sqrt(3)*I/3) + By*(sqrt(3)/3 -I)), -Bz*g3 + Bz*g4]]) Parameters: g1 = -0.3244 ; g2 = 5.761 ; g3 = -7.8904 ; g4 = -13.0138 ; Error of the linear least square method: 6.11e-08 Sum of absolute values of numerical zero elements: 4.12e-03

div.head: TABLE III:
div.p: The computed values of parameters {ai, bi, ci, gi} in Eq. (38) for Na3Bi, obtained from the VASP calculations directly. Here, we compute the band structure of the wurtzite (WZ) InAs semiconductor. Since InAs is usually of ntype, we consider the GM8 conduction bands (doubly degenerate) for the electron doped samples. The matrix representations of generators are presented in TA-BLE E3. The two-conduction-band effective model is constructed in Eq. (41), and the k โข p parameters and gfactors are computed as listed in TABLE V. In Fig. 3(f), the splitting of the conduction bands at field 10T is 6.7 meV, indicating an effective g-factor of 11.6. It is consistent with the experimental value in the bulk material [40,56].
Appendix A: Lรถwdin partitioning theory to obtain k โข p Hamiltonian

The Lรถwdin partitioning theory, also called as quasi-degenerate perturbation, is really a useful and important method to make an approximation to simplify the Hamiltonian by reducing the dimension. The main idea of it is to introduce an anti-Hermitian matrix S, which can transform the origin Hamiltonian matrix H (such as kโขp Hamiltonian H kp defined by Eq. ( 3)) to the optimal Hamiltonian matrix H, which is expressed by

It is obvious that H has the same eigenvalues as H, thus making the corresponding bands all the same. Moreover, H is expected to be block diagonal, as shown in Fig. A1, where A corresponds to the subspace of the bands of interest and B corresponds to the subspace of other bands. After this transformation, we can directly use the matrix block corresponding to A to replace the original Hamiltonian matrix. However, it is difficult to get the analytic or accurate matrix S, so we must use the perturbation expansion method to find the series solution.

First, suppose that the Hamiltonian H = H 0 + H โฒ , where H 0 is a diagonal matrix, which is the main part of the Hamiltonian while H โฒ can be treated as a perturbation. For instance, the k โข p Hamiltonian H kp can be the sum of the diagonal matrix whose diagonal elements are the eigenvalues ฯต n (k 0 )
Extract the small quantities of each order in H N and let them be 0: 1st order [H 0 , S (1) ] + H 2 = 0 (A.9)

Consider the real part and the imaginary part of each elements of U as independent variables, which are denoted as U r11 , U r12 , โข โข โข U rnn and U i11 , U i12 , โข โข โข U inn , respectively. From Eqs. (C.3)-(C.4), it is clear to find that the matrix equations are all linear equations and all the parameters and variables are real. Introduced a column vector where

div.head: Appendix D: Functions of vasp2mat
div.p: Except for the calculation of matrices of generalized momentum ฯ = p + 1 2mc 2 (ล ร โV (r)), spin ล, time reversal operator T and crystalline symmetry operators R, the patch vasp2mat can also do other calculations by setting the parameter vmat in INCAR.mat. All functions of vasp2mat are shown in TABLE D1, where ฯ is Pauli operator. The matrix of time reversal operator T can be calculated with vmat=12; time rev=.true.. At ฮ in the Brillouin zone of InAs, we consider six valence bands for the hole doping sample. The band representations are GM8, GM8 and GM7 in the ascending order.
